question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request that causes `JSONStringify(responseData)` to process a deeply nested or circular-reference-containing object, triggering a synchronous CPU-blocking loop that starves the Node.js event loop and prevents all subsequent transaction confirmation requests from being processed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that results in `res.locals[responseDataLabel]` containing an extremely large object, causing `JSONStringify(responseData)` to allocate gigabytes of memory, exhausting the heap and crashing the Node.js process, resulting in total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the `throw new NotFoundError()` path on line 30 in a tight loop across thousands of concurrent connections, causing the error-handling middleware chain to become saturated and preventing legitimate transaction confirmation requests from being served?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: linkNextHeaderValue()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a pagination `links.next` value containing HTTP header injection characters (CRLF `\\r\\n`) that, when passed to `linkNextHeaderValue(linksNext)` and set via `res.set(LINK_NEXT_HEADER, ...)`, corrupts the HTTP response stream and causes downstream proxies or load balancers to misroute or drop all subsequent transaction requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the spread operator `{...headers.default, ...(headers.path[path] ?? {}), ...(res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] ?? {})}` by injecting a `responseHeadersLabel` value with a prototype-polluted object, causing `res.set(mergedHeaders)` to throw or set malicious headers that crash the HTTP server?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request path that matches a key in `headers.path[path]` containing a null-byte or oversized string, causing `res.set(mergedHeaders)` to throw a native Node.js HTTP error that is not caught, crashing the worker process and halting transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests that each cause `JSONStringify(responseData)` to run synchronously on large payloads, collectively blocking the single-threaded event loop long enough that the network's transaction confirmation heartbeat times out, effectively causing a total network shutdown?
